headlines.title: Inflation hits highest level in nearly 3 years, Fed's preferred gauge shows
headlines.description: Inflation rose for the second consecutive month as the Iran war drove up gas prices.

headlines.title: What Advisors Expect from the New Federal Reserve Chair
headlines.description: Kevin Warsh was confirmed by the Senate earlier this month and officially sworn in as the 17th Chair of the Federal Reserve on Friday.  Instead, advisors are...

headlines.title: It’s Not Just High Gas Prices – Inflation Is Now Spreading Through the US Economy
headlines.description: Inflation is rising and set to become more severe and puts the Fed in a bind.

headlines.title: Goldman Sachs Sees Two Risks Facing the Stock Market—Here's What To Know
headlines.description: Goldman Sachs analysts raised their year-end S&P 500 price target in a note this week, but warned risks to the bull market are growing.

headlines.title: Stock Index Futures Climb on AI Optimism and U.S.-Iran Peace Deal Hopes
headlines.description: June S&P 500 E-Mini futures (ESM26) are up +0.36%, and June Nasdaq 100 E-Mini futures (NQM26) are up +0.56% this morning as optimism surrounding artificial...

headlines.title: Micron’s Cheap Stock Valuation Seen as a Contrarian Warning Sign
headlines.description: (Bloomberg) -- Micron Technology Inc. is the third-best performer in the S&P 500 Index this year and at the same time one of the least expensive stocks in...

headlines.title: Taiwan’s economy is booming thanks to AI. Not everyone sees the benefits
headlines.description: Taiwan is experiencing explosive GDP growth due to chip exports, but some Taiwanese feel left out.

headlines.title: AI And The End Of Recessions As We Know Them
headlines.description: AI could allow the economy to keep growing even as fewer workers benefit from it, raising new questions about GDP and how recessions are defined.

headlines.title: Colombia’s first left-wing gov’t has reduced poverty, but has pile of debt
headlines.description: A big challenge for Colombia's next president will be how to reduce country's debt without sacrificing social gains.

summary.executive_summary:
- Stocks finished firmly higher yesterday despite broad inflation concerns: S&P 500 +0.58%, Nasdaq +0.84%, driven by AI optimism and hopes for a U.S.-Iran peace deal.
- The bond market sent a more cautious signal: the 10-year Treasury yield fell 5.8 basis points (to 4.455%), suggesting investors are pricing in economic slowdown or Fed rate cuts ahead.
- Inflation hit a near three-year high and is broadening across the economy—a headache for the new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who just took office this month.
- Overnight, Asian equities weakened (Hang Seng -1.27%, Nikkei -0.47%) while Europe also dipped, but U.S. futures are climbing this morning (+0.36% on S&P 500 E-Mini) on continued AI strength.
- Gold surged 1.16% and natural gas jumped 8% (likely energy market reaction to Iran tensions), signaling risk-off sentiment beneath the equity rally.
summary.overnight:
- Asian equities mostly soft: Hang Seng fell 1.27% and Nikkei dropped 0.47%, suggesting caution about inflation spillover and potential Fed tightness.
- Europe opened weaker too: FTSE 100 down 0.75%, DAX down 0.34%, tracking the bond market's risk-off mood.
- U.S. futures in full recovery mode this morning: S&P 500 E-Mini +0.36%, Nasdaq 100 E-Mini +0.56%, betting on AI tailwinds and geopolitical stabilization.
- Treasuries extended gains overnight: 10-year yield fell another 5.8 bps to 4.455%, steepening expectations that the Fed may need to cut rates if inflation fears prove overdone or growth slips.
- Dollar weakened slightly (DXY -0.19%) and oil mixed: WTI up 0.25% but Brent down 0.62%, reflecting competing pressures from Iran tensions (supply fear) and demand concerns (higher rates).
summary.central_banks:
- Kevin Warsh is now the Federal Reserve Chair (sworn in this month), and he inherits a thorny situation: inflation rising for the second straight month while markets increasingly expect rate cuts by year-end.
- The inflation surprise is forcing the Fed into a credibility bind. If price pressures persist, Warsh cannot pivot to cuts too quickly without looking reactive; if he stays hawkish, he risks slowdown amid already-weakening global growth signals (Asia equity weakness, bond yields falling).
- No FOMC meeting scheduled today, but the market is clearly repricing Fed expectations lower—bond investors are betting on at least one cut by late 2026 or early 2027.
- The new Fed leadership will likely communicate cautiously until the inflation picture clarifies; watch for any dissent within the incoming leadership on how aggressive to be with rate cuts.
summary.rates_credit:
- The 10-year Treasury yield fell 5.8 bps to 4.455%, a sharp move that reflects investors rotating out of stocks into bonds on inflation concerns and Iran war premium fears.
- The 30-year yield also fell 5.2 bps, but the short end (13-week T-Bill) barely budged (+1.4 bps), flattening the curve slightly—a classic sign of recession worry.
- No auctions scheduled today, but the recent move in yields is setting up a potential "buy on the dip" setup for equities if the inflation surprise fades and geopolitical tensions ease.
summary.geopolitics:
- Iran tensions remain the dominant theme: the conflict drove gas prices higher and is cited as a reason for the recent inflation jump, plus it's fueling hopes for a peace deal (which boosted U.S. futures this morning).
- The peace deal narrative is supporting risk assets, but any escalation could flip sentiment immediately and boost energy prices and safe-haven assets (gold up 1.16% yesterday).
- Taiwan's AI boom is a bright spot (explosive GDP growth from chip exports), but it also concentrates geopolitical risk in one region—any cross-strait escalation would disrupt global semiconductor supply chains and hurt Micron and peers.
- Colombia's debt challenges are background noise for now but worth monitoring if U.S. rate expectations shift, since EM debt becomes costlier when U.S. yields rise.
summary.corporate:
- Micron Technology continues to outperform (third-best S&P 500 performer year-to-date) but is trading at a "cheap" valuation—a contrarian warning sign that the market may be pricing in AI disappointment or cyclical risk.
- AI remains the dominant driver of equity gains: yesterday's Nasdaq strength (+0.84%) and this morning's futures rally (+0.56%) are both heavily weighted toward semiconductor and software names betting on the AI cycle.
- Goldman Sachs raised its year-end S&P 500 target but flagged two major risks—likely relating to inflation (current headwind) and geopolitical uncertainty (Iran)—so bullish on stocks but not without caution.
- No major earnings surprises flagged for yesterday, but the market is clearly rotating: growth and AI names are rallying while defensive sectors (energy, staples) are lagging.
summary.top5:
1. Inflation hits 3-year high, broadening beyond energy—the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge rose for the second straight month, driven partly by Iran war-induced gas prices but increasingly by sticky inflation in other categories. This is the biggest driver of yesterday's bond rally (10-year yield -5.8 bps) and sets up tension for new Fed Chair Warsh.

2. 10-year Treasury yield drops 5.8 basis points to 4.455%—the largest one-day move in recent weeks—signaling that bond investors are pricing in slower growth and/or future Fed rate cuts despite the inflation surprise. This is a major shift from the hawkish posture of just weeks ago.

3. S&P 500 rallies 0.58% on AI optimism and U.S.-Iran peace deal hopes—stocks shrugged off inflation concerns and pushed higher, with Nasdaq 100 outperforming (+0.84%), suggesting tech and chip names are driving the session and the market is betting on geopolitical de-escalation.

4. New Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh sworn in amid inflation headwinds—the transition coincides with upside inflation surprise, creating immediate policy pressure and uncertainty about Warsh's stance on future rate moves. Market expectations for cuts have risen materially.

5. Gold surges 1.16% and natural gas jumps 8%—safe-haven and energy assets moved sharply higher, a sign of underlying risk-off sentiment (inflation, geopolitical uncertainty, demand concerns) that contradicts the equity rally and suggests investors are hedging.
summary.daily_pitch:
TRADE: Short EUR/USD
DIRECTION: Short
RATIONALE: The U.S. 10-year yield fell 5.8 bps yesterday while European yields remained broadly flat (DAX weakness suggests ECB may be even more dovish than the Fed). This rate differential should weigh on EUR/USD, which ticked up 0.30% yesterday but is fighting a headwind. Additionally, the U.S. dollar weakness is thin (-0.19% DXY) and vulnerable to reversal if inflation remains sticky—the Fed will need to stay higher for longer, supporting the dollar. A short from 1.165 targets 1.150 over the next few days as rate expectations stabilize.
RISK: Any escalation in U.S.-Iran peace deal negotiations could trigger a broad dollar weakness and risk-on rally, lifting EUR/USD quickly.
